pages.title: Overview
pages.body: Physics Particles is a focused introduction to Artlab's particle physics stdlib. A fountain of glowing blue particles streams upward from a point on the ground plane, arcs under gravity, and fades out over a three-second lifetime. Clicking anywhere on the canvas ray-casts against an invisible `THREE.Plane` at ground level and teleports the emitter to that world-space position, giving the user direct spatial control with just a few lines of raycasting boilerplate. The example deliberately keeps all logic in the three lifecycle functions so the stdlib API is easy to read in isolation.

pages.title: World, Emitter & Click Handler
pages.body: A `createParticleWorld` call allocates the physics simulation state; the returned world object is passed to `emitter()` along with the Three.js scene so the stdlib can manage the `THREE.Points` object internally. Emitter parameters — rate, speed, spread angle, lifetime, color, size, and gravity — are all set at construction time as a plain config object rather than individual setter calls. The click handler uses a `THREE.Plane` at `y = GROUND_Y` as the intersection target instead of raycasting against the visible ground mesh, which avoids the need to make the ground mesh part of the hit-test set and works correctly even at oblique camera angles.

pages.title: Force Field & Emitter Tick
pages.body: Each frame, `forceField` applies an upward impulse centered slightly above the emitter origin, giving particles a gentle additional lift that keeps the fountain shape coherent even as gravity pulls them back down. The force magnitude scales by `dt` to remain frame-rate-independent. `ctx._emitter.update` is then called with the elapsed time and delta time; internally it spawns new particles at the configured rate, integrates positions under gravity, ages particles, and removes any whose lifetime has expired — all without any manual loop in this file.

pages.title: Teardown
pages.body: Teardown removes the click listener, calls `ctx._emitter.dispose()` to release the particle system's geometry and GPU buffers, and manually disposes the ground plane's geometry and material before removing it from the scene. The explicit disposal pattern here is intentional — it serves as a model for how Artlab examples should clean up after themselves when the stdlib does not own all scene objects.
